title: Diversification and the stock-bond split
deck: Stocks pay more in the long run but swing wider in the short. Bonds smooth the ride at the cost of some return. A real portfolio is a mix of both, tuned to the investor's time horizon. The closing section of the book.
objectives: Identify the trade-off between risk and expected return; explain why a diversified portfolio combining stocks and bonds reduces volatility; relate the stock-bond mix to the investor's time horizon.

content.text: Stocks have historically delivered about 10% per year on average over long periods — the highest reliable return of any major asset class. Bonds have returned roughly 5% per year on average. The math seems obvious: if stocks pay twice what bonds do, why hold bonds at all? The answer is in the year-to-year experience. The 10% stock return is an average across many decades; in any individual year, stocks can be up 30% or down 30%. A 100% stock portfolio worth $100,000 at the start of 2007 had fallen to roughly $63,000 by early 2009 — a 37% drawdown that took until 2013 to fully recover. Bonds drifted gently up over the same period.

Real investors hold both. Stocks for long-run growth; bonds for short-run predictability and to fund any near-term spending without selling stocks at a low. The right mix depends on the investor's time horizon — the number of years until the money will be needed — and on individual tolerance for volatility. This closing section develops the risk-return trade-off, the arithmetic of asset-allocation mixes, and the standard rules of thumb for tuning the mix to a life stage. It is also the closing section of the book.
7.6.1  Risk, return, and the trade-off

Three properties together describe any major asset class. The expected return is the long-run average annual return: stocks ~10%, bonds ~5%, cash ~2-3%. The volatility is the year-to-year variation around that average (the standard deviation introduced in Section 5.3): stocks swing the most, bonds some, cash almost not at all. The two are tightly linked — higher expected return almost always comes paired with higher volatility, a phenomenon called the risk-return trade-off.

Diversification is the practice of holding multiple asset classes so that no single one dominates the portfolio's behavior. A 60% stock, 40% bond portfolio (often shortened to “60/40”) has an expected return that is the weighted average of the two component expected returns: 0.60 × 10% + 0.40 × 5% = 8%. Its volatility is much closer to the bond side than to the stock side, because the two asset classes often move in different directions during short-run market events, partially canceling each other's swings.

content.caption: Figure 7.6. The risk-return trade-off. Three sample portfolios on the trade-off curve: 100% bonds (low risk, ~5% return), a 60/40 stock-bond mix (medium risk, ~8% return), and 100% stocks (high risk, ~10% return). The investor chooses a point on the curve based on time horizon and tolerance for short-run swings.

content.text: Definition 7.6. Diversification is the practice of spreading investments across multiple asset classes (stocks, bonds, cash, real estate, etc.) to reduce the portfolio's overall volatility. The asset allocation is the set of percentages assigned to each class; the time horizon is the number of years until the money is needed and is the single largest factor driving the appropriate stock-bond mix.
7.6.2  Time horizon and the rule of thumb

A 25-year-old saving for retirement at 65 has a 40-year horizon; they can afford to ride out even the worst stock-market drawdowns, because they will not need the money for decades. An appropriate allocation for such an investor might be 90% stocks, 10% bonds. A 65-year-old already drawing on the portfolio cannot afford a 40% drawdown that takes six years to recover, because they need to fund this month's grocery bill; an appropriate allocation might be 40% stocks, 60% bonds. The shift from a stock-heavy young portfolio to a bond-heavy older one is one of the most important transitions in personal financial planning.

A common rule of thumb prescribes the stock allocation as a simple linear function of the investor's age: percent in stocks = 110 − age. A 30-year-old should hold 80% stocks; a 60-year-old should hold 50%; a 70-year-old should hold 40%. The rule produces a slowly-declining stock allocation as the investor ages, without requiring market timing or active management. Target-date retirement funds, the dominant product in 401(k) plans, encode roughly this glide path internally.
Choose the asset allocation. Everything else — which stocks, which bonds, which fund — is secondary by an order of magnitude.

Margin noteThe risk-return trade-off, the asset-allocation framework, and the time-horizon rule of thumb were developed in the mid-20th century by economists including Harry Markowitz (Nobel laureate, 1990), William Sharpe (Nobel laureate, 1990), and others. Their work, collectively known as modern portfolio theory, is the academic foundation behind every 401(k) target-date fund, every robo-advisor, and most professional investment-management practice. The math is more sophisticated than this textbook can develop in one section, but the core insight is the diversification effect: combining asset classes reduces portfolio volatility without proportionally reducing expected return.

Common mistakeChoosing an allocation based on recent market performance rather than time horizon. Stocks looking strong over the last few years tempts investors to load up on stocks at exactly the wrong moment (just before a correction). Stocks looking weak over the last year tempts investors to flee bonds at exactly the wrong moment (just before a recovery). The correct approach is to choose an allocation that fits your time horizon and tolerance for volatility, and to stick with it across market cycles. “Set it and forget it” is dull advice, but it is also the most-supported recommendation in the academic literature on personal investing.

Example 7.6. Choosing allocations for two investors.

Investor A is 30 years old and saving for retirement at 65. Investor B is 60 years old and plans to retire at 65. Using the rule of thumb stocks % = 110 − age, suggest an allocation for each. Then compute the expected return of each portfolio, assuming stocks return 10% and bonds return 5%.

Solution.

Investor A. Stocks % = 110 − 30 = 80%; allocation is 80% stocks, 20% bonds. Expected return = 0.80 × 10% + 0.20 × 5% = 8% + 1% = 9%.

Investor B. Stocks % = 110 − 60 = 50%; allocation is 50% stocks, 50% bonds. Expected return = 0.50 × 10% + 0.50 × 5% = 5% + 2.5% = 7.5%.

Investor A's higher expected return comes with substantially higher volatility, which she can tolerate because she will not draw on the portfolio for 35 years. Investor B's lower expected return is paired with much lower volatility, which is what he needs as retirement begins.

In the wildThe dominant retirement-savings vehicle in the United States is the 401(k), an employer-sponsored account that lets workers contribute pre-tax income (deferring federal income tax from Section 7.2 until retirement) and often receive a matching contribution from the employer. The default investment in most 401(k) plans is a target-date fund: a single fund that automatically rebalances its stock-bond mix from stock-heavy to bond-heavy over the years leading up to the worker's planned retirement date. The default is generally a good one; participants who simply contribute to the default target-date fund and never look at the account again often do better than those who actively manage their allocation.
Exercises

content.problem: A portfolio is 60% stocks (10% expected return) and 40% bonds (5% expected return). What is the portfolio's expected return?
Using the rule of thumb stocks % = 110 − age, what allocation would you recommend for a 45-year-old investor?
A retiree chooses a 20% stock, 80% bond allocation. With stocks returning 10% and bonds 5%, what is the portfolio's expected return?
Why do most investors hold both stocks and bonds rather than just the higher-returning stocks alone?
content.answer: Bonds reduce the portfolio's overall volatility and provide stability when near-term spending needs would otherwise force selling stocks at a low. Stocks deliver higher long-run returns but with wider year-to-year swings; combining the two produces a more tolerable ride for investors with shorter horizons.

content.problem: True or false: the asset-allocation decision (the stock-bond mix) is a less important driver of long-run portfolio performance than the choice of individual stocks within the stock allocation.
content.answer: False. Asset allocation accounts for the vast majority of long-run portfolio variance; individual stock selection within the stock allocation is a much smaller effect. This is one of the most-replicated findings in academic finance.

content.problem: Compute the expected return of a portfolio that is 70% stocks (10% expected return), 25% bonds (5%), and 5% cash (3%).
Using the **110-minus-age** rule of thumb, what stock-bond allocation is recommended for: (a) age 25, (b) age 50, (c) age 70?
content.answer: (a) Age 25: 85% stocks, 15% bonds. (b) Age 50: 60% stocks, 40% bonds. (c) Age 70: 40% stocks, 60% bonds.

content.problem: A portfolio is **80% stocks, 20% bonds**, totaling **$200,000**. Stocks return 8% in a year, bonds return 3%. What is the total portfolio return in dollars and as a percent?

content.text: Closing the book

This section closes the book. Across seven chapters we have built a complete financial-literacy toolkit: linear functions and percent change as the algebraic foundation; simple and compound interest as the math of money growing over time; amortization as its mirror image for borrowing; descriptive and inferential statistics for reading data; probability and expected value for reasoning under uncertainty; and the income-tax and stock-market machinery of this final chapter for navigating an adult financial life.

The themes recur. The compound-interest formula from Chapter 3 is the same exponential growth that drives long-run stock returns in this chapter. The progressive-bracket math from Section 7.2 is structurally a percent-change cascade, the same form that appeared in budgeting (Section 2.6) and savings yield (Section 3.4). The expected-value formula from Section 6.5 underpins the “expected return” vocabulary of asset allocation in Section 7.6. The Law of Large Numbers from Section 6.6 is why long-run average stock returns are reliable while individual-year returns are not. Mathematics is unified; the same handful of tools recur in different applied domains throughout the course.

Beyond any exam, the math built across these seven chapters is durable. The next time you read a poll headline, calculate a tip, sign a lease, take out a loan, fund a retirement account, or read an investment quote, you will be doing it with the full apparatus of college mathematics behind you. The applied competence is the goal; the chapter-by-chapter mechanics were the means.
More worked examples

content.title: The 110-minus-age rule of thumb
content.problem: Using the **110-minus-age rule**, what stock-bond allocation would be recommended for a **30-year-old** investor? For a **60-year-old**?
content.solution:
**Apply the rule.**

Stock allocation ≈ 110 − age, with the remainder in bonds.

**Age 30:** stocks ≈ 110 − 30 = **80%**; bonds ≈ **20%**.

**Age 60:** stocks ≈ 110 − 60 = **50%**; bonds ≈ **50%**.

The intuition: as retirement approaches, the portfolio shifts toward lower-volatility bonds, reducing the risk of a bad market year wiping out a large chunk of savings right before you need to spend them.

*Caveat.* This is a starting point, not a prescription. Real allocation depends on goals (early retirement vs working to 70), risk tolerance (a 30-year-old might still want 50% bonds for sleep-at-night reasons), and other holdings (real estate, business equity, pension).

content.title: Why diversification reduces risk
content.problem: Suppose stocks have an expected return of **9%** with **high** volatility, and bonds have an expected return of **4%** with **low** volatility. A 60/40 portfolio holds 60% stocks, 40% bonds. What is the portfolio's expected return, and why is the portfolio less *volatile* than holding only stocks?
content.solution:
**Compute the weighted-average expected return.**

Expected return = 0.60 × 9% + 0.40 × 4% = 5.4% + 1.6% = **7%/year**.

That's lower than stocks alone (9%), but only by 2 percentage points — and the volatility is significantly reduced.

*Why volatility drops.* Stocks and bonds often move differently in response to the same economic news. When stocks fall on bad earnings news, high-quality bonds often *rise* as investors flee to safety, partially offsetting the stock loss. The combined portfolio swings less than its stock-only counterpart.

*The trade.* You give up some expected return for smaller drawdowns and a smoother ride. For investors close to retirement — when a 30% drop is hard to recover from — this is a very favorable trade. For young investors with decades of horizon, the smoother ride may not be worth the lost return.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Diversification — splitting investments across stocks and bonds (and other asset classes) — reduces volatility without proportionally reducing expected return. Rule-of-thumb allocations like 110 − age give a starting point; personal goals, risk tolerance, and time horizon adjust the actual mix. The 60/40 stock/bond portfolio is a classic balanced baseline.

content.title: Target-date funds and the age-based glide path
content.content: Every 401(k) plan in America now defaults new employees into a **target-date fund** — a fund named for your retirement year (e.g., "Fidelity 2065 Target Date Fund" for a 22-year-old today). The fund automatically reallocates from heavier-stock to heavier-bond as you age, applying exactly the kind of glide path discussed in this section. By default, no math is required from the saver. But knowing the underlying logic — *why* the allocation shifts — is what lets you decide whether the default fits your goals or whether you should actively choose a different mix.

content.text: 7.6
